questions.question:
Consider a binary tree T in which every node has either zero or two children. Let n > 0 be the number of nodes in T. Which ONE of the following is the number of nodes in T that have exactly two children?

A. (n-1)/2
B. n/2
C. n/4
D. (n+1)/2
questions.to_find: Number of nodes with exactly two children in a full binary tree with n nodes

understand.plain: We have a special binary tree where every node is either a leaf (zero children) or has exactly two children. Given the total number of nodes n, we need to figure out how many of those nodes have exactly two children. The key insight is the relationship between internal nodes and leaf nodes in such a tree.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where every node has either 0 or 2 children (no node has exactly 1 child).
keywords.example: A root with two leaves is a full binary tree with 3 nodes.

keywords.explain: A node that has children (degree > 0). In a full binary tree, every internal node has exactly 2 children.
keywords.example: In a full binary tree with 7 nodes, there are 3 internal nodes and 4 leaves.

keywords.explain: A node with no children. Also called an external node.
keywords.example: In the tree root->left, root->right where left and right have no children, left and right are leaves.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='200' cy='30' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>I</text><circle cx='100' cy='100' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>I</text><circle cx='300' cy='100' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='106' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L</text><circle cx='50' cy='170' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L</text><circle cx='150' cy='170' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='176' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>L</text><line x1='200' y1='48' x2='100' y2='82' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='200' y1='48' x2='300' y2='82' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='118' x2='50' y2='152' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='118' x2='150' y2='152' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>n=5, I=2=(n-1)/2, L=3=(n+1)/2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A full binary tree with 5 nodes: root (internal), two subtrees where left child is internal with two leaf children, and right child is a leaf. Shows I=2=(n-1)/2 and L=3=(n+1)/2.

given.aim: Find the count of nodes with exactly two children in a full binary tree of n nodes.

terms.meaning: Total number of nodes in the full binary tree (n > 0)
terms.example: n = 7 for a complete full binary tree of height 2
terms.connects: We need to express the answer (number of two-child nodes) in terms of n

terms.meaning: Number of internal nodes (each having exactly 2 children)
terms.connects: This is exactly what we want to find

terms.meaning: Number of leaf nodes (having zero children)
terms.connects: L = I + 1 in any full binary tree, and n = I + L

given.plan: Use the fundamental property of full binary trees: in any full binary tree, the number of leaves L = number of internal nodes I + 1. Combined with n = I + L, we solve for I in terms of n.

steps.title: State the key property of full binary trees
steps.apply: In any full binary tree (every node has 0 or 2 children), the number of leaf nodes exceeds the number of internal nodes by exactly 1. This is a well-known result proven by induction: base case (single node) L=1, I=0 gives L=I+1. Each time we expand a leaf into an internal node with 2 children, I increases by 1 and L increases by 1 (net +1 leaf). So L = I + 1 always holds.
steps.note: This property only holds for full binary trees, not arbitrary binary trees.

steps.title: Solve for I in terms of n
steps.apply: Total nodes: n = I + L. Substituting L = I + 1: n = I + (I + 1) = 2I + 1. Solving for I: 2I = n - 1, so I = (n - 1) / 2. Since n must be odd for a full binary tree (n = 2I+1 is always odd), (n-1)/2 is always a whole number.
steps.note: The number of nodes with exactly two children = I = (n-1)/2.

solution.result: The number of nodes with exactly two children is (n-1)/2. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Key property of full binary trees: if L = leaf nodes and I = internal nodes, then L = I + 1 and total n = 2I + 1, giving I = (n-1)/2.

questions.question:
Consider the following tree traversals on a full binary tree:
i. Preorder
ii. Inorder
iii. Postorder

Which of the following traversal options is/are sufficient to uniquely reconstruct the full binary tree?

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (ii) and (iii)
C. (i) and (iii)
D. (ii) only
questions.to_find: Which combination(s) of traversals can uniquely reconstruct a full binary tree

understand.plain: For a general binary tree, we need any two traversals (preorder+inorder or inorder+postorder) to reconstruct it uniquely. However, for a FULL binary tree (where every node has 0 or 2 children), we have extra information — any single traversal plus the 'full' property, or preorder+postorder, is sufficient. The question asks which of the listed pairs suffice.

keywords.explain: Binary tree where every node has exactly 0 or 2 children. This extra constraint helps distinguish subtrees.
keywords.example: Preorder: ABC, Postorder: BCA uniquely gives root A with left leaf B and right leaf C.

keywords.explain: Given traversal sequences, can we uniquely determine the tree structure and node values?
keywords.example: Inorder+preorder always reconstruct a general binary tree uniquely.

keywords.explain: For full binary trees, preorder and postorder together uniquely reconstruct the tree, unlike general binary trees.
keywords.example: For full tree with root A: preorder A,B,C and postorder B,C,A gives unique tree.

understand.visual_alt: For a full binary tree with n nodes, any two traversals among preorder, inorder, postorder suffice to uniquely reconstruct the tree. Preorder+Postorder (option C) works specifically because of the full binary tree constraint.

given.aim: Identify which traversal pairs can uniquely reconstruct a full binary tree.

terms.meaning: Visit root, then left subtree, then right subtree
terms.example: For tree with root A, left B, right C: preorder is A, B, C
terms.connects: First element is always the root; helps identify root of each subtree

terms.meaning: Visit left subtree, then root, then right subtree
terms.example: For same tree: inorder is B, A, C
terms.connects: Splits nodes into left and right subtrees once root is identified

terms.meaning: Visit left subtree, then right subtree, then root
terms.example: For same tree: postorder is B, C, A
terms.connects: Last element is always the root; helps identify root of each subtree

given.plan: Check each pair: (i)&(ii) preorder+inorder always works for any binary tree. (ii)&(iii) inorder+postorder always works for any binary tree. (i)&(iii) preorder+postorder works for general trees only when all node values are distinct — and for full binary trees, this is always sufficient. (ii) only: inorder alone is never sufficient since we cannot determine root position.

steps.title: Check option A: Preorder + Inorder
steps.apply: Preorder gives the root as its first element. Inorder then splits remaining nodes into left and right subtrees. This reconstruction works for ANY binary tree (not just full), so it is sufficient. Option A is CORRECT.
steps.note: This is the classical reconstruction algorithm that works generally.

steps.title: Check option B: Inorder + Postorder
steps.apply: Postorder gives the root as its last element. Inorder then splits remaining nodes into left and right subtrees. This reconstruction works for ANY binary tree, so it is sufficient. Option B is CORRECT.
steps.note: Symmetric to preorder+inorder — also a classical result.

steps.title: Check option C: Preorder + Postorder
steps.apply: For a general binary tree, preorder+postorder is NOT sufficient (ambiguity when a node has only one child). However, for a FULL binary tree, every internal node has exactly two children. Given preorder and postorder of a full binary tree, the second element of preorder is the root of the left subtree, and we can find it in postorder to determine the split. This works recursively and is sufficient for full binary trees. Option C is CORRECT.
steps.note: The full binary tree constraint eliminates the ambiguity that would exist for general binary trees.

steps.title: Check option D: Inorder only
steps.apply: Inorder traversal alone cannot identify which node is the root. Multiple different trees can produce the same inorder sequence (e.g., a right-skewed tree and a left-skewed tree can have the same inorder). Option D is INCORRECT.
steps.note: No single traversal alone is sufficient to uniquely reconstruct any non-trivial binary tree.

solution.result: Options A, B, and C are sufficient to uniquely reconstruct a full binary tree. Answer: A, B, C.

questions.formula_note: Classical tree reconstruction: preorder+inorder or inorder+postorder work for any binary tree; preorder+postorder works specifically for full binary trees due to the absence of single-child nodes.

questions.question:
Let H, I, L, and N represent height, number of internal nodes, number of leaf nodes, and the total number of nodes respectively in a rooted binary tree. Which of the following statements is/are always TRUE?

A. L <= I + 1
B. H + 1 <= N <= 2^(H+1) - 1
C. H <= I <= 2^(H-1)
D. H <= L <= 2^(H-1)
questions.to_find: Which inequalities relating height, internal nodes, leaf nodes, and total nodes always hold for any rooted binary tree

understand.plain: We have a rooted binary tree (not necessarily full or complete) and four quantities: H=height, I=internal nodes, L=leaf nodes, N=total nodes. We need to check which of the four given inequality statements are always true for ANY binary tree — including degenerate (path-like) trees and full trees.

keywords.explain: The number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf (some definitions use number of nodes; here H=0 means single node).
keywords.example: A single node has H=0; a tree with root and one child has H=1.

keywords.explain: A node that has at least one child.
keywords.example: In a path of 4 nodes, the first 3 are internal (each has one child), the last is a leaf.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where each internal node has exactly one child — essentially a linked list.
keywords.example: Root -> A -> B -> C: height=3, I=3, L=1, N=4.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='60' y='20' class='lbl ink'>Path tree (H=3)</text><circle cx='60' cy='50' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='60' cy='95' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='60' cy='140' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='60' cy='185' r='15' class='bg'/><line x1='60' y1='65' x2='60' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='110' x2='60' y2='125' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='60' y1='155' x2='60' y2='170' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='85' y='55' class='sub mut'>I=3, L=1, N=4</text><text x='240' y='20' class='lbl ink'>Full tree (H=2)</text><circle cx='280' cy='50' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='200' cy='110' r='15' class='hi'/><circle cx='360' cy='110' r='15' class='bg'/><circle cx='160' cy='170' r='15' class='bg'/><circle cx='240' cy='170' r='15' class='bg'/><line x1='265' y1='62' x2='215' y2='98' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='295' y1='62' x2='345' y2='98' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='188' y1='123' x2='172' y2='157' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='212' y1='123' x2='228' y2='157' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='260' y='195' class='sub mut'>I=2, L=3, N=5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Left: degenerate path tree with H=3, I=3, L=1, N=4. Right: partial full tree with H=2, I=2, L=3, N=5. Used to verify inequalities.

given.aim: Verify which of the four inequality statements always hold for any rooted binary tree.

terms.meaning: Length of longest root-to-leaf path in edges
terms.example: Single node: H=0; path of 4 nodes: H=3
terms.connects: Bounds the minimum and maximum number of nodes in the tree

terms.meaning: Count of nodes with at least one child
terms.example: Path of 4 nodes: I=3 (each except the last has one child)
terms.connects: Related to L via L <= I+1 for binary trees

terms.meaning: Count of nodes with no children
terms.example: Path of 4 nodes: L=1 (only the last node)
terms.connects: Related to I and N via L <= I+1 and N = I+L

given.plan: Test each statement with extreme cases (degenerate path tree and full/complete binary tree) to check if the inequality holds in all cases. A counter-example proves an inequality is not 'always true'.

steps.title: Check A: L <= I + 1
steps.apply: For any binary tree (internal nodes have 1 or 2 children): count the edges. Each internal node contributes at most 2 edges downward. Each non-root node receives exactly 1 edge. So 2*I >= (N-1) edges (where N-1 = total edges). Also N = I + L, so total edges = I + L - 1. Each internal node has at most 2 children: sum of children <= 2I. But sum of children = N - 1 = I + L - 1. So I + L - 1 <= 2I, giving L <= I + 1. This is ALWAYS TRUE.
steps.note: Equality L = I + 1 holds for full binary trees; L < I + 1 when some internal nodes have only one child.

steps.title: Check B: H + 1 <= N <= 2^(H+1) - 1
steps.apply: Lower bound: A tree of height H must have at least H+1 nodes (the path from root to deepest leaf has H+1 nodes). So N >= H+1. Upper bound: A complete binary tree of height H has at most 2^(H+1) - 1 nodes (levels 0 through H, each filled: 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + 2^H = 2^(H+1) - 1). So N <= 2^(H+1) - 1. Both bounds are ALWAYS TRUE.
steps.note: The minimum N=H+1 is achieved by a degenerate (path) tree; the maximum 2^(H+1)-1 by a perfect binary tree.

steps.title: Check C: H <= I <= 2^(H-1)
steps.apply: Lower bound H <= I: For a path tree of height H, there are H edges, meaning H+1 nodes, and I = H (all except the leaf are internal). So I = H, which satisfies I >= H. For a full tree, I can be larger. The lower bound seems to hold. Upper bound I <= 2^(H-1): For H=2, a full binary tree has I = 1 + 2 = 3 internal nodes, but 2^(H-1) = 2^1 = 2. So 3 > 2, which VIOLATES the upper bound. Option C is FALSE.
steps.note: Counter-example: perfect binary tree of height 2 has I=3 but 2^(H-1)=2, so I > 2^(H-1).

steps.title: Check D: H <= L <= 2^(H-1)
steps.apply: Lower bound H <= L: For H=1 (root with two children), L = 2 and H = 1, so L >= H holds. But wait, for H=1 with root and ONE child (degenerate), L = 1 and H = 1, so L = H. But what about H=2 degenerate? L=1, H=2, so 1 < 2 — this VIOLATES L >= H. So D is FALSE.
steps.note: Counter-example for D: degenerate tree of height H=2 (root->A->B) has L=1 but H=2, so L < H.

solution.result: Statements A and B are always true. Answer: A, B.

questions.formula_note: Binary tree bounds: for any binary tree, L <= I+1 (leaf-internal relation) and H+1 <= N <= 2^(H+1)-1 (height-size bounds). These are fundamental properties used in tree analysis.

questions.question:
Which one of the following binary trees has its inorder and preorder traversals as DCAD and ADCD, respectively?

A. A tree with A as root, D as left child of A, C as right child of A, D as left child of C (inorder: D,A,D,C -> wait reconstructing)
B. (See image for tree diagrams)
C. (See image for tree diagrams)
D. (See image for tree diagrams)
questions.to_find: Which binary tree among the options has inorder traversal DCAD and preorder traversal ADCD

understand.plain: We are given two traversal sequences: inorder = DCAD and preorder = ADCD. We need to reconstruct the binary tree from these and match it against the given options. The first element of preorder is always the root, and inorder tells us which nodes are in left vs right subtrees.

keywords.explain: Root -> Left subtree -> Right subtree. The first node in preorder is always the root.
keywords.example: If preorder is ADCD, then A is the root of the entire tree.

keywords.explain: Left subtree -> Root -> Right subtree. Finding the root in inorder splits left and right subtrees.
keywords.example: If root is A and inorder is DCAD, then nodes before A (i.e., DC) are in left subtree, and nodes after A (i.e., D) are in right subtree.

keywords.explain: Using preorder and inorder sequences together to uniquely rebuild the tree structure.
keywords.example: Preorder ADCD: root=A; inorder DCAD: A at position 2 (0-indexed), so left subtree has nodes DC and right subtree has node D.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 400 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='200' cy='30' r='20' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='37' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><circle cx='100' cy='100' r='20' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='107' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><circle cx='300' cy='100' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='107' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>D</text><circle cx='150' cy='170' r='20' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='177' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>C</text><line x1='183' y1='47' x2='117' y2='83' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='217' y1='47' x2='283' y2='83' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='115' y1='117' x2='138' y2='153' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Inorder: D,C,A,D | Preorder: A,D,C,D</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Reconstructed binary tree: root A, left child D (with right child C), right child D. Inorder traversal gives D,C,A,D and preorder gives A,D,C,D.

given.aim: Reconstruct the binary tree from preorder=ADCD and inorder=DCAD, then identify which option matches.

terms.meaning: The preorder traversal sequence of the target binary tree
terms.example: First element A is the root
terms.connects: Gives root of each subtree recursively

terms.meaning: The inorder traversal sequence of the target binary tree
terms.example: A appears at index 2 (0-indexed), so 2 nodes (D, C) are in left subtree, 1 node (D) is in right subtree
terms.connects: Splits nodes into left and right subtrees once root is known

terms.meaning: After identifying root and splitting inorder, apply the same logic recursively to left and right subtrees
terms.example: Left subtree: preorder=DC (next 2 from preorder), inorder=DC

given.plan: Step 1: Root = first of preorder = A. Step 2: Find A in inorder: position 2, so left inorder = DC (2 nodes), right inorder = D (1 node). Step 3: Left subtree preorder = next 2 from preorder = DC; Right subtree preorder = last 1 = D. Step 4: Recurse on left subtree (preorder=DC, inorder=DC) and right subtree (preorder=D, inorder=D).

steps.title: Identify the root from preorder
steps.apply: Preorder = A, D, C, D. The first element is A, so A is the root of the entire tree.
steps.note: Always start with the first element of preorder to find the root.

steps.title: Split inorder around the root to find left and right subtrees
steps.apply: Inorder = D, C, A, D. Root A is at index 2 (0-indexed). Nodes before A: {D, C} -> left subtree (2 nodes). Nodes after A: {D} -> right subtree (1 node). Left subtree has 2 nodes, so from preorder, next 2 elements (D, C) belong to the left subtree. The remaining element (D) belongs to the right subtree.
steps.note: The count of nodes in the left subtree from inorder determines how many elements from preorder (after the root) belong to the left subtree.

steps.title: Reconstruct left subtree
steps.apply: Left subtree: preorder = D, C and inorder = D, C. Root = D (first of preorder). In inorder D, C: D is at index 0, so no left subtree for D, and right subtree = {C}. Right subtree of D: preorder = C, inorder = C, so it's a single leaf node C. Result: D is the root of left subtree with C as its right child.
steps.note: D has no left child and C as its right child.

steps.title: Reconstruct right subtree and assemble full tree
steps.apply: Right subtree: preorder = D, inorder = D. Single node, so D is a leaf (right child of root A). Final tree structure: A is root, A's left child is D, D's right child is C, A's right child is D (a leaf). Verification: Inorder of this tree: left(A)=inorder(D with right C)=D,C then root A then right(A)=D -> D,C,A,D = DCAD. Preorder: A, then preorder(left)=D,C, then preorder(right)=D -> A,D,C,D = ADCD. Both match.
steps.note: The tree has root A, left subtree rooted at D (with right child C), and right child D.

solution.result: The binary tree with root A, left child D (which has right child C), and right child D matches inorder DCAD and preorder ADCD. This corresponds to option A.

questions.formula_note: Binary tree reconstruction algorithm using preorder and inorder: root = preorder[0], find root in inorder to split left/right subtrees, recurse. Time complexity O(n^2) naive, O(n) with hash map.
